justification: All 4 business_impact sub-fields (financial, operational, user, risk) are substantively populated with multi-sentence analysis. stakeholders_affected lists 7 distinct groups with specific impact per group (far exceeds the 3 minimum). 4 escalations raised for missing information (service tier scope, ETA accuracy data, operational definition, enterprise SLA contracts). Contractual SLA risk is explicitly identified in BR-3, business_impact.risk, and a dedicated escalation. confidence.level is 52, well below the 80 threshold, with detailed assumptions listed. Competitive positioning is mentioned in business_impact.risk ('Reputational risk if competitors maintain precise ETAs') and in the sales team stakeholder impact.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough business_impact analysis across all 4 dimensions with nuanced trade-offs (e.g., operational benefit to drivers vs. cost to support team)
7 stakeholder groups identified with specific, differentiated impacts — goes well beyond the minimum
4 well-structured escalations with options and recommendations, covering the exact contractual SLA gap called out in the rubric
Confidence level of 52 is well-calibrated given the missing contract information, with clear reasoning for the low confidence
Edge cases are excellent — same-day delivery, perishable goods, midnight boundary, KPI inflation, and timezone issues all identified
BR-5 correctly identifies the risk of masking operational degradation behind reduced precision — a subtle but critical insight
Competitive positioning mentioned in both risk assessment and sales team stakeholder impact

trials.justification: All 4 business_impact sub-fields (financial, operational, user, risk) are populated with substantive, multi-dimensional analysis. stakeholders_affected lists 8 distinct groups with specific impact per group, far exceeding the 3 required. Multiple escalations for missing information (service tier scope, presentation vs data model, ETA accuracy data, contractual SLAs). Contractual SLA risk explicitly identified in both the risk field and a dedicated escalation. confidence.level of 45 is well below the 80 threshold, with detailed assumptions and low-confidence areas. Competitive positioning mentioned in the risk field ('Competitive risk in markets where rivals offer precise delivery windows').

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough stakeholder analysis with 8 groups including non-obvious ones like Finance/SLA compliance, API partners, and Marketing
Confidence level of 45 is well-calibrated — appropriately low given the missing contract and accuracy information
Four well-structured escalations covering service tier scope, presentation vs data model, ETA accuracy, and contractual SLAs
Financial analysis balances both cost reduction (fewer false SLA breaches) and revenue risk (premium tier degradation, B2B churn)
Operational analysis correctly identifies the dual benefit/cost: reduced support contacts but loss of internal forcing function for time precision
Competitive positioning explicitly addressed with Amazon-style delivery windows as comparison
Edge cases are exceptionally strong — midnight boundary, timezone differences, same-day tier, B2B receiving hours, metric inflation
Goes beyond rubric requirements with business rules, acceptance criteria, and domain language definitions that demonstrate deep logistics domain expertise
Correctly identifies that on-time rate KPI inflation could mask real operational problems — a subtle second-order effect

trials.justification: All 6 pass criteria met: (1) All 4 business_impact sub-fields (financial, operational, user, risk) contain substantive multi-sentence analysis. (2) stakeholders_affected lists 7 distinct groups with specific impact descriptions per group. (3) 4 escalations raised for missing information (service tier scope, display-vs-calculation, business motivation, B2B contract audit). (4) Contractual SLA risk explicitly identified in BR-4, business_impact.risk, and a dedicated escalation. (5) confidence.level=45, well below 80, with detailed assumptions listed. (6) Competitive positioning mentioned in business_impact.risk ('if competitors offer time-specific ETAs, this change makes the service appear less capable') and in stakeholders for Sales.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough stakeholder breakdown with 7 groups including non-obvious ones like Drivers/Couriers and Product/Analytics
confidence.level of 45 is well-calibrated — appropriately low given the missing contract and tier information
Escalation for contractual SLA risk is precisely what the rubric requires — Bird explicitly flags inability to assess without contract data
Financial analysis covers both upside (reduced SLA penalties, premium upsell) and downside (conversion rate risk, contractual disputes) — not one-dimensional
Edge cases are excellent — the midnight boundary case, same-day meaninglessness, API breaking change, and historical metric invalidation all demonstrate deep domain reasoning
BR-4 and BR-5 correctly distinguish between invariant and non-invariant business rules, showing nuanced domain judgment
Escalation questioning the business motivation (hiding poor OTD vs genuine accuracy alignment) shows strategic thinking beyond surface-level analysis
Operational impact correctly balances the benefit (reduced pressure) against the cost (eroded discipline, degraded metrics utility) as the rubric requires
